_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: Her coin built the first global economy — and the world still hasn't paid her back.

museum_objects.title: Chop-Marked 8 Reales Coin
museum_objects.description: A Spanish colonial silver 8-reales coin stamped repeatedly with Chinese merchant chop marks, proof it circulated across Asia.
museum_objects.significance: Every chop mark on this coin is a stranger, somewhere on the other side of the world, deciding they trusted Camila's silver enough to stamp their own name on it too.
museum_objects.museum: Smithsonian National Museum of American History
museum_objects.plain_language: An old Mexican silver coin covered in small stamps added by Chinese traders — proof this coin traveled all the way around the world and back.
museum_objects.institution: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

resolved.via: TIER 2 — free API key required (CEO signup)

display_alternatives.title: An account of the recent epidemic of puerperal fever as it appeared in the Dublin Lying-in Hospital (IA b30562077).pdf

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Codex Mendoza
museum_objects.description: An Aztec pictorial and Spanish-annotated manuscript detailing tribute, economy, and daily life in early colonial Mexico.
museum_objects.significance: Before the peso, there was this — a record of an entire economy of tribute and trade that the Spanish silver dollar would eventually replace and erase.
museum_objects.plain_language: An illustrated Aztec record book showing how the economy worked right before Spanish silver coins took over.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Florentine Codex
museum_objects.description: A monumental illustrated ethnographic work documenting Aztec culture, compiled under Spanish friar Bernardino de Sahagún.
museum_objects.significance: This is the last full portrait of a world before Camila's silver economy swallowed it whole.
museum_objects.plain_language: A giant illustrated encyclopedia of Aztec life, made right as Spanish silver money was taking over the region.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Casa Nacional de Moneda de Potosí
museum_objects.description: The colonial mint building in Potosí, Bolivia, where much of the silver that became Spanish colonial pesos was struck.
museum_objects.significance: This building turned mountain silver into global money — and worked its laborers to death doing it. Camila carries that weight in every coin she represents.
museum_objects.museum: UNESCO World Heritage Site / Casa Nacional de la Moneda
museum_objects.plain_language: The actual mint building in Bolivia where much of the silver for Spanish colonial coins was turned into money.
museum_objects.institution: UNESCO World Heritage Site / Casa Nacional de la Moneda

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.title: Macuquina (Cob Coinage)
museum_objects.description: Roughly hand-cut irregular silver coins produced in colonial Spanish American mints, predecessors to the milled 8-reales.
museum_objects.significance: Crude, uneven, hand-cut — but every irregular edge on these coins carried real silver value across oceans and empires.
museum_objects.accession_number: Referenced in Banco de México archive
museum_objects.plain_language: Rough, hand-cut silver coins made in the earliest days of Spanish colonial money in the Americas.
museum_objects.accession: Referenced in Banco de México archive

world_map.map_description: Camila's silver peso was struck in Mexico City and Potosí, then sailed the Manila Galleon route to become the first truly global currency, trusted from Boston to Beijing.

nodes.label: Mexico City (Casa de Moneda)
nodes.description: Site of the colonial mint that struck Spanish silver pesos for over two centuries.

nodes.label: Potosí, Bolivia
nodes.description: The mountain of silver that funded the entire Spanish colonial economy and much of early global trade.

nodes.label: Manila, Philippines
nodes.description: Terminus of the Manila Galleon trade route, where Mexican silver pesos were exchanged for Chinese silk and porcelain.

nodes.label: Acapulco
nodes.description: The Pacific port from which silver-laden galleons departed for Asia every year.

nodes.label: Fujian, China
nodes.description: Region where enormous quantities of Spanish silver pesos entered the Chinese economy, becoming a preferred trade currency.
